Before continuing, it is important to adjust the rendering view. To do this: In the <3D Viewer> panel, click the " pin icon" and select "Center View" ( Image 3.II ). In the same expanded menu, activate < Orthographic Rendering > to prevent objects from being visually distorted. To do this, click "Toggle between orthographic and perspective rendering". You are in orthographic mode when the scale icon becomes highlighted and clickable. To optimize the view for the future landmark placement, turn off " 3D Cube " and " 3D Axis Labels " from the " eye-icon " menu. Image 3.II. Volume rendering setup in 3D Slicer .

Then, place the 11 landmarks . To standardize landmark placement, minimize intra-observer error and increase the accuracy of the missing landmark estimation process; it is highly recommended to consult the landmark positioning procedure shown in the supplementary video go to step #24 , as well as to follow the guidelines provided in Table 2 . These landmarks can be classified into two types : those required to guide the acquisition of the cross-sections and define the cross-sectional reference plane -here referred to as the Alveolar Reference Plane ( ARP )-, and those necessary for performing the size standardization process. The theoretical explanation of how the ARP is defined is provided in go to step #8.1 . Coordinate format and system when using external software The OrientCSG workflow described here is designed to read two coordinate formats: the Amira landmark-coordinate format used in this route, and the 3D Slicer Markups-style coordinate format described in the Slicer route. If landmarks were collected in another software package, the coordinate table must first be converted to one of these two formats before running orient_mandible() . The converted table should follow the examples shown in this protocol, especially with respect to landmark order and the columns containing the X, Y, and Z coordinates. You may use a script, large language AI models or other automated tools to reformat coordinates. A second precaution concerns the coordinate system . See go to step #9.4 for a detailed explanation of this issue.

Landmarks for ARP definition and cross-sections acquisition. Place the landmarks (“LM” from now on) 1 to 8. To achieve this: Set the <3D Viewer> panel to fullscreen by double-clicking inside the viewer. Go to the <Module Finder> box and select " Markups ". In the <Create Markups> section, select " Point List " ( Image 4.II ). A new point list will be created and displayed in the < Node > panel below. Rename the list if desired. Click on the new point list. The cursor should change to the landmark placement mode . Place the first landmark, LM1 , in its anatomical position. Repeat the process until LM1 - LM8 have been placed, always following the order shown in Table 2 and Images 1, 4 and 5 . To place each new landmark, click " Place a control point " ( Image 4.II ). This icon is only available when the point list is selected. If needed, create an orthogonal visual reference by placing one vertical and one horizontal measurement line through the approximate center of the specimen. This can help landmark placement, but it is not required. Image 4.II. Landmark placement in 3D Slicer using the Markups module. To ensure accuracy, always adhere strictly to the landmark order outlined in this guide . OrientCSG identifies each landmark based on its position in the sequence. For example, the first point in the dataset will always be interpreted as LM1 , regardless of its actual anatomical position. Failing to follow the specified order will therefore result in calculation errors .

Landmarks for size-standardization. Place LMs 9 to 11 following the same procedure and using the same LM dataset as in the previous step. LM10 and LM11 do not necessarily need to be located on the same anatomical side. However, if they are placed on opposite sides, this must be explicitly indicated later when running OrientCSG ( go to step #9 )

Once the 11 landmarks are correctly positioned, copy their coordinates . To do this: In the <Markups> module, open the <Control Points> tab ( Image 4.II ). Select all rows. For the 11-landmark protocol, the table should contain 11 rows. Copy the selected rows using "Ctrl+C" and keep them in the clipboard. These coordinates will be pasted into R in the next step.

In this step, the landmark coordinates generated in 3D Slicer are processed with the R package OrientCSG in order to: Geometrically estimate LM1_Line Reconstruct the Alveolar Reference Plane ( ARP ) Compute the orientation elements required for CS1, CS2, and CS3 Calculate the linear mandibular measurements Generate Python command blocks for 3D Slicer , which will be used to orient the ARP, cross-sectional slices, and camera views. If this is your first time using R , we strongly recommend that before proceeding, you review some introductory tutorials to the program that clarify its basic functions, such as those offered by Rstudio Education . RStudio is recommended for convenience, but it's not required (you can run these commands in any R environment).

Launch RStudio or open the R console ( R version 4.0 or higher is recommended). Then run the installation command displayed in the note below (this step is necessary only the first time you use the package). install.packages("remotes") remotes::install_github("Alex-Martin1/OrientCSG") library(OrientCSG)

Copy the following command into a new R script . Paste the landmark coordinates copied in go to step #8.3 between the quotation marks, next to landmarks_str <- line. It is essential that the coordinates follow the landmark order defined in Table 2 . Then, set the specimen ID by replacing SPECIMEN_ID between the quotation marks in the individual_id argument of " orient_mandible() " function. You may use any name here. # 1. Load OrientCSG ==== library(OrientCSG) # 2. Paste landmark coordinates ==== # Paste below the coordinates exported from 3D Slicer. landmarks_str <- " PASTE YOUR LANDMARK COORDINATES HERE "# Coordinates must follow the selected landmark order. # 3. Run mandibular orientation ==== res <- orient_mandible( landmarks_str = landmarks_str, individual_id = "SPECIMEN_ID", camera_distance_mm = 300, lm1_side = "RIGHT", complete_arch = FALSE, estimate_lm10 = FALSE, lm9_valid = TRUE, lm_coord_system = "LPS", #Change this to lm_coord_system = "RAS" only if the pasted values have been verified as RAS world coordinates. SLICER = TRUE, volume_name = "VOLUME_NAME" ) # 4. Inspect outputs ==== res res$summary res$measurements # 5. Print 3D Slicer Python blocks ==== cat(get_slicer_py(res, section = "CS1")) cat(get_slicer_py(res, section = "CS2")) cat(get_slicer_py(res, section = "CS3")) # 6. Copy 3D Slicer Python blocks to the clipboard ==== copy_slicer_py(res, section = "CS1") copy_slicer_py(res, section = "CS2") copy_slicer_py(res, section = "CS3")

For the default 11-landmark workflow, the remaining arguments of orient_mandible() can be left as shown in the script. The arguments that may need to be modified depending on specimen preservation and landmark placement are: Use " complete_arch = FALSE " when LM1_Line is estimated geometrically from LM1 , LM2 , LM3 , and LM4 . Use " complete_arch = TRUE " only in complete-arch workflows, when LM4 is the real LM1_Line . Use " lm9_valid = TRUE " when LM9 was placed on a real anatomical gonion. Use " lm9_valid = FALSE " only when LM9 is a placeholder inserted to preserve the input structure. In that case, OrientCSG still uses the available landmarks to orient the cross-sections, but returns measurements depending on LM9 , including corpus length and bigonial breadth , as non-computable. Use " estimate_lm10 = FALSE " when LM10 and LM11 were placed on the same anatomical side. Use " estimate_lm10 = TRUE " when LM10 and LM11 were placed on opposite anatomical sides. In that case, OrientCSG reflects LM10 across the relevant symmetry plane (see Image 4 ) and computes mandibular length as LM10_Line - LM11 . This option is not applicable to 9-landmark workflows, because LM10 and LM11 are absent. camera_distance_mm defines the camera distance used in the 3D Slicer 3D verification view. It affects visualization, not the anatomical calculations. lm1_side specifies the anatomical side on which LM1 was placed. Use lm1_side = "RIGHT" when LM1 was placed on the right mandibular corpus , and lm1_side = "LEFT" when LM1 was placed on the left one. This argument is used to orient the generated views consistently for CS1, CS2, and CS3. It does not change the anatomical position of the cross-sections, but it controls the viewing side and the anatomical direction of the generated camera views. Some options (as " complete_arch ") only need to be changed when using alternative landmark configurations or preservation workflows, as described in the corresponding step-cases. Geometrically estimated landmarks and measurements should be used with caution. Estimated points such as LM1_Line , LM9_Line , and LM10_Line are derived from symmetry or reflection procedures and therefore introduce additional uncertainty compared with direct measurements between preserved anatomical landmarks. Whether these estimations are acceptable depends on specimen preservation, study aims, and the level of measurement uncertainty considered appropriate by the researcher. The magnitude of the error introduced by these estimations will be evaluated in a dedicated methodological study currently in preparation.

Once the arguments have been selected according to the preservation and landmarking conditions of the specimen, run the remaining lines of the script until reaching " # 6. Copy 3D Slicer Python blocks to the clipboard ". The three copy_slicer_py() lines can be run at this stage without causing any problem, but they will need to be run again later from go to step #10 onward, one at a time, when each python block is pasted into 3D Slicer . Therefore, keep the R session open while continuing with the next steps. After running the script, an object named "res" will be created in R . The script will print two main tables in the R console: " res$summary " ( Table 4 ), which contains the geometric components of the computed landmarks, points, and vectors. " res$measurements " ( Table 5 ), which contains the linear mandibular measurements that may be used later for size standardization ( go to step #23 ). The status and method columns indicate whether each measurement was directly computed, estimated, or not computable. If you are working in RStudio , these tables can also be opened with View() , for example View(res$summary) . This displays the outputs in a spreadsheet-like viewer within the RStudio environment. Save the coordinates and measurements in your preferred format and environment, for example as .csv files or in an Excel spreadsheet. Table 4. OrientCSG geometric output for mandibular cross-section orientation. Table 5. OrientCSG linear mandibular measurements (in mm) for size assessment and standardization

This step explains the procedure for capturing Cross-Section 1 (CS1) . To achieve this: In RStudio , run the first line of the section "# 6. Copy 3D Slicer Python blocks to the clipboard": copy_slicer_py(res, section = "CS1"). This will copy the python command block required to orient CS1 directly to your clipboard. Return to 3D Slicer. Ensure that the volume object is correctly named, matching the name introduced in volume_name argument in go to step #9.3 . Click on the " Show Python Console " icon ( Image 5.II ) in order to open the console. Right-click on the "input command" line and press "Control + V" to paste the command block into the console panel. After running the python block, 3D Slicer should automatically orient the < Red slice viewer > to CS1 , configure the <3D Viewer> for verification of the section orientation, display the ARP , show the estimated LM1_Line , and add a 10 mm scale reference. These new objects can be inspected in the <Node> panel of the <Data> module, where their visualization can be turned off, if needed, by clicking the corresponding "eye" icon. To ensure that the process has been completed successfully, check the following in the <3D Viewer>: The <3D Viewer> is shown in orthographic view and provides a coherent verification of the cross-section orientation. The "Red slice plane" intersects LM5 and LM6 . The "Red slice plane" is perpendicular to the ARP . The created ARP object visually represents the Alveolar Reference Plane . Image 5.II. Cross-sectional slice CS1 aligned to obtain a fully orthogonal view. Sections appearing upside down OrientCSG ( v0.3.4 , not older versions) orients the generated views from anatomical landmark information rather than from the original scanner axes. The right-left direction is defined from the side on which LM1 was placed, specified with lm1_side. The anteroposterior direction is derived from the relationship between LM2 and the LM1 - LM1_Line axis. In practical terms, LM2 provides the anterior reference used to orient the mandibular arch relative to the transverse axis. The superior-inferior direction is derived from the ARP normal, whose sign is oriented using the available inferior reference landmarks. OrientCSG first prioritizes a real LM9 when the gonion is preserved ( lm9_valid = TRUE ). If this is not available, LM3 or LM4 may provide the inferior reference depending on the selected landmarking workflow. In complete-arch workflows without a preserved gonion, the placeholder LM9 is used as the inferior reference. For this reason, placeholder LM9 should still be placed on the best available inferior mandibular reference. Then, if a section appears upside down, mirrored, or viewed from the unexpected side, first check that: LM1 was placed on the side indicated by lm1_side . The landmarks were pasted in the correct order. lm_coord_system matches the coordinates actually pasted into R . LM9 was placed on a real gonion, or, when the gonion is not preserved, on a plausible inferior mandibular reference with lm9_valid = FALSE . The generated section intersects the expected anatomical region and is perpendicular to the ARP. A wrong view orientation does not necessarily mean that the cross-sectional plane was incorrectly defined. If the section passes through the intended region and remains perpendicular to the ARP, but the captured view is still rotated by 180°, the image can be rotated later during post-processing , for example in Fiji-ImageJ . Restoring the standardized view After running the python block, 3D Slicer prints a helper command that can be used to recover the standardized view if it is lost. If you move the view, change the zoom, or accidentally alter the visualization, run restore_view() in the python console. This command restores the standardized view generated for the current cross-section.

Set the view before capturing the cross-sectional image. To do this: Ensure that the section correctly intersects the region defined by LM5 and LM6 ( Image 6.II ). Return to the standardized view by running restore_view() in the python console. After running this command, the <Red slice viewer> may occasionally show a visually distorted section. This should be corrected by setting the <Red slice viewer> to fullscreen . To do this, double-click anywhere inside it. Ensure that the section includes the smallest possible amount of dental tissue . If the section cuts through a substantial part of a dental root, use the <Translate> bar ( Image 7.II ) to move the section slightly to a nearby location where less dental tissue is included. This tool changes the position of the red slice plane, but does not change its orientation. If the section position was modified with the <Translate> bar, move the scale reference to the new section position by running refresh_orientcsg_scale() in the Python Interactor. Using the 3D Slicer ruler instead of the OrientCSG scale If you prefer to use the scale generated by 3D Slicer instead of the scale created by the Python command, activate the ruler from the <Red slice viewer> . To do this, click the <Extended options> icon (>>) ( Image 5.II ), then click the ruler icon and select the ruler display option you prefer ( Image 5.II ). The advantage of the 3D Slicer ruler is that it automatically follows changes in the slice view. However, it is less customizable than the scale created by the Python block. If you prefer to use the 3D Slicer ruler, you can turn off the scale generated by the command in the <Data> module, in the <Node> panel. To do this, click the eye icon next to OrientCSG_scale_CS1_10mm and OrientCSG_scale_CS1_label . Image 6.II. Top view of the CS1 capture process showing the cross-sectional plane intersecting landmarks 5 and 6 .

Capture and save the cross-sectional image. To do this: Adjust the brightness, contrast, and colormap settings until you are satisfied with the visualization. Try to avoid excessive brightness in the enamel and ensure that the cortical bone is clearly visible. Zoom in until you are satisfied with the view to be captured. Click on the < Capture screenshot > icon ( Image 7.II ). Save the image using a consistent naming convention . We recommend using "individual_CSX", where "individual" is the specimen ID and "X" is the section number. If you want to save the image as a TIFF file, include the .tiff extension in the file name. Otherwise, 3D Slicer will save the image as a PNG file by default. For example, for specimen "39" and section CS1, use: 39_CS1.tiff Click " Save As... ", select the preferred folder, and save the image. Image 7.II. Cross-sectional image capture process.

This step explains the procedure for capturing Cross-Section 2 (CS2) . The process is identical to that of CS1, with the only difference being the two landmarks the slice must intersect. This is because the cross-sectional plane of CS2 shares the same orientation as CS1 along its Z-axis (superoinferior), while it differs slightly along its X-axis (mediolateral), as it must intersect landmarks 7 and 8 instead of 5 and 6 . To achieve this: Return to R or RStudio and run the second line of the section "# 6. Copy 3D Slicer Python blocks to the clipboard": copy_slicer_py(res, section = "CS2"). This will copy the python command block required to orient CS2 directly to your clipboard. Return to 3D Slicer and open the <Console> . Press "Control + V" to paste the command block into the <Console>. Once the standardized view has been obtained, continue from go to step #10.1 . Save the image using a different name, for example "39_CS2.tiff". The resulting view should show: The "Red slice plane" passes through the region defined by LM7 and LM8 . The "Red slice plane" is perpendicular to the ARP . The ARP visually represents the Alveolar Reference Plane . The <3D Viewer> is shown in orthographic view and provides a coherent verification of the CS2 orientation ( Image 8.II ). When the python command block for CS2 is executed, the same objects previously used to orient and capture CS1 are reconfigured to capture CS2. If you wish to preserve the CS1 objects , rename them beforehand (for example, by adding a "1" at the end of each object name), and run the command block again. Image 8.II. Cross-sectional slice CS2 aligned to obtain a fully orthogonal view.

This step explains the procedure for capturing Cross-Section 3 (CS3) , which provides information related to the mandibular symphysis . Its acquisition differs from CS1 and CS2 because it is not guided by a pair of landmarks located on the mandibular corpus. To achieve this: Return to R or RStudio and run the third line of the section "# 6. Copy 3D Slicer Python blocks to the clipboard": copy_slicer_py(res, section = "CS3"). This will copy the python command block required to orient CS3 directly to your clipboard. Return to 3D Slicer and open the <Console> . Press "Control + V" to paste the command block into the <Console>. Once the standardized view has been obtained, continue from go to step #10.1 . Save the image using a different name, for example "39_CS3.tiff". Read the note below. The resulting view should show: The "Red slice plane" passes through the region defined by LM2 . The "Red slice plane" is perpendicular to the ARP and to the LM1-LM1_Line vector. The ARP visually represents the Alveolar Reference Plane . The resulting view will be generated from the side specified in the lm1_side argument The <3D Viewer> is shown in orthographic view and provides a coherent verification of the CS3 orientation ( Image 9.II ). Image 9.II. View from the right side of the CS3 capture process, showing the cross-sectional plane intersecting LM2 and oriented according to the LM1–LM1_Line direction. These steps should be systematically repeated for each specimen in our sample, capturing as many sections as feasible in each instance. Avoid cross-sections that exhibit orientation impossibility, fragmented bone, absence thereof, or antemortem tooth loss. It is strongly recommended to organize the output by creating dedicated folders for each individual, naming each folder using the assigned individual label used in your study.

This step provides a theoretical explanation of how landmark placement defines the Alveolar Reference Plane (ARP) . Landmark placement is used not only to record anatomical reference points, but also to define the ARP , which provides the reference plane for orienting the mandibular cross-sections. To define a plane in Euclidean space, at least three non-collinear points are necessary. In this workflow, where only one dental quadrant is assumed to be preserved, LM1_Line (or LM1' ) is not directly landmarked but estimated geometrically. It is computed as the symmetrical reflection of LM1 across a reference plane P , defined by LM2 , LM3 , and LM4 ( Image 3 ). Image 3. Graphical representation of landmark 1_Line calculation process. The calculation of landmark 1_Line involves a series of geometric operations that can be performed manually but are more efficiently handled with the R package OrientCSG , which automates the calculation of this point, generates the geometric elements required for cross-section orientation, and computes the linear measurements used for later size standardization.

Some arguments in the script shown in go to step #9.2 are essential for the 3D Slicer route: SLICER = TRUE specifies that " orient_mandible() " must generate Python command blocks for 3D Slicer . The default is SLICER = FALSE , which generates TCL command blocks for the Amira route. lm_coord_system specifies the coordinate convention of the landmark values pasted into R . For further information, read the note below. Coordinate-system clarification In medical imaging, it is useful to distinguish between the image coordinate system, the world coordinate system, and the anatomical coordinate convention used by each software environment. This distinction matters here because Amira and 3D Slicer use different anatomical coordinate conventions : the Amira route uses an LPS ( Left Posterior Superior )-like convention, whereas 3D Slicer works internally in RAS ( Right Anterior Superior ) world coordinates. When coordinates are copied manually from a 3D Slicer Markups table or exported from a Markups file, they may be pasted or written in LPS convention, even if the "Markups" table displays R / A / S columns. In this common case, set " lm_coord_system " to " LPS " or do not include the argument. Use " lm_coord_system = "RAS" " only when the coordinates pasted into R are verified RAS world coordinates. On the other hand, OrientCSG performs the geometric calculations in an internal LPS-like coordinate system, and the argument lm_coord_system tells the package the coordinate system of the input landmark coordinates. If SLICER = FALSE , " orient_mandible() " emits TCL blocks for Amira in the LPS -like convention. Therefore, if the input coordinates are RAS ( lm_coord_system = "RAS" ), the package first converts them to the internal LPS-like system. On the other hand, if SLICER = TRUE , " orient_mandible() " emits Python blocks for 3D Slicer in RAS world coordinates. Therefore, the output is converted to RAS before the Python blocks are generated. The conversion between LPS and RAS is a 180° rotation around the z axis, equivalent to transforming ( x , y , z ) into (- x , - y , z ).

In " orient_mandible() ", replace volume_name = "VOLUME_NAME" with the exact name of the mandibular volume loaded in 3D Slicer . For example, following the specimen shown in Image 3.II , this argument could be written as volume_name = "Indv_39". The name between quotation marks must match the 3D Slicer volume name character by character. You can check this name in the < Data > module, in the < Node > panel. The volume is the node represented by the cube icon . This argument is important because it tells the Python block which 3D Slicer object must be controlled during cross-section extraction. If the volume name is incorrect, the Python command will try to interact with an object that does not exist and will return an error when executed.

From this point onward, the workflow no longer depends on whether the cross-sectional images were generated using Amira or 3D Slicer. The remaining steps of the protocol are identical for both software routes. Once the cross-sectional images have been captured and saved, continue with go to step #15 to extract the CSG properties and apply the size-standardization procedure.
